item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given that it's an etching on flat blackness, the concept of the measurable level pretty much goes down to how small of a physical probe can feel the edges. They aren't sure it scales down infinitely, just smaller than they can tell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
while the back is lightly etched with a fractal pattern which shrinks below the measurable level near the center. SCP-2196-a absorbs all radiation directed at it,

If it doesn't reflect any radiation, how was anyone able to perceive that the etching is fractal? Did they use a tunneling electron microscope on it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know what red-shifting is, so I assumed that blue-shifting meant that something was getting closer.

The comments proved my suspicions.

You get a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understood how everything here worked once I read the comments, but I'm not really finding it all that effective. The problem is that the important parts are too vague, while the bits which are blatant are, as Kalinin put it, cliches.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't get it until now. Now I like it, the concept is great. But if I had to dig through the comments to understand it and enjoy it, then I think it doesn't really deserve an upvote. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a collection of tired site tropes:

  • "Thing what fucks with the Foundation." I feel like there so many of these now. These always come across as weirdly moralistic and it implies that the Foundation really sucks at the whole secrecy thing if like five dozen different groups know of its existence. I can explain the logical issues with that at great length if I must.
  • "Thing what compels you." This one's a twofer. Not only does it make you interact with it, which is an extremely cheap source of horror/unease/etc., it also forces you to react in a paranoid and distressed manner. I've always found this kind of thing incredibly lazy. The thing is scary because it says right here that it's scary and it will make you scared. No room is left for the piece to affect characters and the reader in a natural way.
  • "A GoI did it." I frequently see people shopping drafts in the forums and the chat who reach a point where they begin casting about for which GoI they think should be involved somehow for some reason. They usually have between 2-3 choices and then settle on something because a GoI should be involved, by their reckoning. I don't like GoIs at all (again, I can expound on that at great length elsewhere), but the SH reference here feels shoehorned and pointless. It doesn't really add anything to this except a "hey I referenced this thing" moment.
  • "Helpful explanatory note." Ugh. This one can't die soon enough. So many things come with little notes pinned to them or conveniently located near them or sent somehow by anomalous FedEx to the site where they're at. There are other narrative means of explaining the provenance of the thing if that information must be conveyed. Please, everyone, use them. And in this case, why would you even leave a note like this? "I should provide some information on this, but I should probably make it cryptic-sounding so that only a vague impression is communicated."

The "it's coming to get us" line at the end isn't bad with the way the red and blue shift was handled, but that's not nearly enough to salvage this in my reading. It's hit a checklist of things that many other articles have done before, and as a result it feels like about a few hundred other articles as I read it. I think we need to push ourselves a little more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read the skip.
And again.
Then I paced around my desk going: OK. We have disk, fractal, smooth, blueshift, white dot, radiation-absorbing, curiosity, compulsion, ….
What's that make?
Er.
<synapses working so hard I can't hear myself thinking>
Um. Nothing?
Okay. -> comments.

Sorry, enemyofentropy. This doesn't work for me. IMO, there aren't enough clues to get to the conclusion you're aiming at without help.
Also, every time I want to enlarge an SCP picture to have a closer look, no enlargement is possible… So I didn't try with this one until I read the comments… which didn't really help either, because my eyesight is so bad I cannot make out what that little dot is. Oh, wait… A mouse? That's what I see!

Downvote from me. BUT, I hasten to add that writing this for a short contest is, I believe, wasting it, because the basic idea is very interesting, and you already achieve a lot of healthy WTF??? within the contest parameters. I'd love to see this expanded!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the upvote! I'm glad you appreciate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I started reading it I thought "heh, making the Foundation curiosity against themselves, thats cool".

Then wjen the SH got involved I went full "okay, what the heck is this thing?"

And then read the last addendum…

<slow clap> Congratulation sir. This is just great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
- hide block

Not just any something travelling at near light speed, this is something that not only makes you break your brain looking at it, but is visible at several light-years distance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Me too. And once I'd started reading, I had that guilty "Oh, crap!" moment when I realized I'd fallen into the trap.

+1 for excellently suspending disbelief.

Edit: Did notice the blueshift…something that was moving away at near-c is now coming back at a similar speed. Wonder what the mass is when it arrives? Talk about a relativistic time-bomb!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh.

Huuuuuh.

Hmm.

Consider it flipped.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ spoilers
- show

Its a bit less complex than that, the concept was that the SH found SCP-2196-b, and it scared them so much that they made a fractal pocket dimension (-a) that temporarily contains -b though sheer, dumb distance (hence the laser signal bounce back a few years later) and passed it off the the masters of containment, the foundation. The second addendum shows that whatever -b is, it has turned around and started coming back towards the surface, at speeds high enough to cause visible blueshift.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

So it's some kind of black hole or space-time doodly that's getting shorter? Like, it's a black tube with a mirror at the end, and it's light-years long, but the mirror is getting gradually closer to us? To put it in a very, very simplistic manner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
- hide block

I get it, there are a lot of curiosity/compulsion skips already out there. This may help if the final addenda wasn't clear: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blueshift

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I might be willing to flip my vote, but I've decided I'm not no-voting contest entries, so you're -1 from me at the moment.

I just kind of don't get what's going on in the final addenda. I sense that it's the explanation of the whole thing, but said explanation eludes me. Also, I'm kind of tired of "it makes you curious" things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Regarding the Serpent's Hand connection, I'm seeing two possibilities:

1. They know something about this thing we don't, and they're terrified. They gave it to the Foundation to contain because it's actually something Keter class, but the Foundation calls it Euclid because they don't know the whole story.

2. They know something about this thing we don't, and they're using it against us.

Without knowing for what the motivations are, I'm having trouble with this one. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do know how the SH tie-in works

If you ask me, since SH is highly decentralised, I would imagine a real diverse lot under the SH banner. They could assist [INSERT GoI], just as much as to oppose [INSERT GoI]. If there was a common point, it could be that Foundation = Jailors and GOC = Bookburners

Oh, please do. I would love a Tale about it. I think that some lesser entries will benefit from Tales, a la the older SCPs driven more than Tales mentioning it than on itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's vaguely weird, and vaguely creepy, but not enough of either of those things for an upvote. Novote it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll admit it, before I read the whole document, I enlarged the picture to get a better look at the added dot.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
- hide block

First off thank you for pointing it out, lowercase site-66 is in fact a typo.
In regards to the recovery, if I was not writing for the contest, I would have expanded it, and I do know how the SH tie-in works, but I hit the word limit and had to make something short. Perhaps I will write a tale about it once the competition is over. For now, thank you for the feedback, and I respect your vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Weird coincidence. I was looking at the Wikipedia page that picture is from just a few hours ago.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

site-66

Call it a pet peeve, but "site-66" should be capitalised as a proper noun.

Pros:

  • The poem is a nice touch. The SH link this SCP offers adds on to the diversity of SH SCPs, which also show the outright diversity of said GoI.

Cons:

  • There are similar SCPs that affect Foundation personnel's stances and even geared to target aspects of the Foundation's standard actions.
  • The recovery comes off as somewhat too convenient. I personally do not buy the SH link. It works out of universe, and may be in-universe (i.e. Jailors), but that is more of a hunch in-universe than something that has its place in the article.

Verdict: Downvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't have much to say about this one. It's got an old-school creep vibe with a subtle hint of impending doom to it, and I must admit to enjoying it when the Foundation's obsession with study gets turned against it. +1.

item.title: Thanks and Image credit
item.content:encoded:
Many thanks to Doctor Cimmerian, psul, Psychostick, Zolgamax, Cris_To_Baal, and many others for your critique.

Image is used under Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike license,
courtesy of the wikimedia foundation.
